interview_name: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins

main_summary.summary: Joint interview recorded May 28, 2011 in Bogalusa, Louisiana, with Geraldine Crawford Bennett, her niece Toni Breaux (Willie Exposé Breaux), and her nephew Willie Elliot Jenkins (known as Chuck). The three recall the Bogalusa civil rights movement through the work of Gayle Jenkins, Bennett's sister and the mother of Breaux and Jenkins. Gayle Jenkins served as secretary-treasurer of the Bogalusa Voters League alongside president A.Z. Young and vice-president Robert Hicks, and worked closely with CORE organizers and the Deacons for Defense during the 1965 campaign. The witnesses describe segregated life in Poplas Quarters, school integration through the Willie E. Jenkins v. Bogalusa School Board suit that placed Chuck in Bogalusa Junior High in fall 1967, the senior-class walkout at Central Memorial High in 1965, the 1967 march from Bogalusa to Baton Rouge with violence at Satsuma, Klan attacks on the Jenkins home, Gayle Jenkins's twenty-seven-year tenure on the Bogalusa City School Board, and a fifty-thousand-dollar buyout offer from Governor John McKeithen that Jenkins refused. Bennett practiced as a nurse in Covington for twenty-seven years after Bogalusa hospitals refused to hire her.
key_themes: women's organizing in the Black church tradition
main_summary.historical_significance: Direct family testimony about Gayle Jenkins, a central but under-documented organizer of the Bogalusa movement, provided by her sister, daughter, and son. The interview supplements the better-known accounts of A.Z. Young, Robert Hicks, and Charles Sims by foregrounding the women's organizing infrastructure behind the public male leadership and the household cost of sustained Klan threats. Specific named referents include the 1967 Bogalusa-to-Baton Rouge march at Satsuma, the school integration suit Willie E. Jenkins v. Bogalusa School Board, attorney Richard Sobol's legal work, the New Orleans CORE-affiliated law firm of Collins, Douglas, and Elie, and Governor John McKeithen's buyout offer.

chapters.title: In the Home of the Jenkins Family
chapters.summary: Joe Mosnier opens the Civil Rights History Project interview in Bogalusa, Louisiana, on May 28, 2011, with curator Elaine Nichols, filmmaker John Bishop, and Dr. Rickey Hill present, and after a recording restart introduces the participants: Gayle Jenkins's son Willie Elliott Jenkins known as Chuck, her sister Geraldine Crawford Bennett, and her daughter Willie Exposé Breaux known as Toni.

chapters.title: My Sister Was Born a Go-Getter
chapters.summary: Geraldine Bennett describes segregated Bogalusa and the white clinic where she worked as maid and her sister Gayle cooked, a born leader whose drive to send their two daughters to college pushed them into the movement, and Toni adds how Gayle refused a politician's money and was shaped by the story of her own grandmother's white father, Mr. Smith, who would not give the light-skinned girl a nickel in Poplas Quarters.

chapters.title: Cooking Breakfast for CORE
chapters.summary: Toni remembers the saddest part of the 1960s movement, being sent to relatives like her Aunt Sis because CORE workers and the Klan kept people guarding the house, going by day to cook grits for CORE, and her quiet stepfather Monroe Jenkins, a mill worker who joked he could not fetch Gayle from jail because he was watching the game.

chapters.title: We Want Our Freedom
chapters.summary: Toni describes her 1965 senior year at the all-black Central Memorial High School, when the students marched off campus singing We Shall Overcome to the Freedom Hall on Sullivan Drive against Principal J.C. Crump's warning, and her brother Willie E. Jenkins becoming the named plaintiff in Willie E. Jenkins v. Bogalusa School Board.

chapters.title: The Backbone of the Movement
chapters.topic: A Domineering Mother and Forgotten Hero
chapters.summary: Toni recalls the white and black proms, her domineering mother overruling Grambling to send her to Dillard for nursing though she became a teacher, pressing hair for home customers and earning a diamond ring for never needing to be asked, and calls her mother the forgotten hero and backbone of the movement who founded the Voters League with A.Z. Young, R.T. Young, and Bob Hicks but, unlike the men who won promotions, took nothing for herself.

chapters.title: You're Going to Get Your Freedom
chapters.summary: Chuck remembers his sister pulling him from seventh grade for his first march and bar counting, his mother whom he thought mean ordering him to file the school-integration suit, and telling her he hated all white folks until she answered that her own mother was white, while he caught hell from the black community for attending the white school.

chapters.title: Certified but Not Hired
chapters.topic: Her Nursing Denied and Her Businesses
chapters.summary: Geraldine explains that Gayle, repeatedly rejected and jailed, finally earned her nursing degree in New Orleans only to be passed over for a Bogalusa job and to commute her whole career to Covington twenty-eight miles away, and the family lists her enterprises, the hair work, rent houses, a store, and a snowball stand the children had to run, contrasting the chance-taking mother with calm Monroe, who met the dark house and unpaid light bill with the line, try paying the bill.

chapters.title: That's What Brought Gayle In
chapters.summary: Geraldine and Toni tell how Monroe, brave after a Friday-night nip, put his pocketknife to a Klansman's neck to stop CORE workers being killed on Fourth Street, drawing death threats that brought the Deacons for Defense to escort him to the mill and led Gayle to take over once Monroe sobered up and grew nervous.

chapters.title: Sitting Outside Crying to Get Out of Jail
chapters.summary: Asked to situate the houses, Geraldine recalls a jail incident with a hosepipe hooked to hot water and Chuck crying outside because they had put him out of jail, then maps black Bogalusa, Poplas Quarters, Richardson Town, the Bogue projects and River Terrace, against the white neighborhoods divided by the railroad tracks, and the bare white Freedom House on Sullivan Drive.

chapters.title: A Man Ought to Be Up Front
chapters.summary: Toni recalls the January 1965 leadership change as Andrew Moses left the Voters League amid charges he was an Uncle Tom and Gayle asked A.Z. Young to take over, and explains her mother's belief, rooted in scripture and a church upbringing, that a man should lead, alongside her sense that the white power structure would always promote a black woman before a black man.

chapters.title: Name the Dog Deacon
chapters.summary: Chuck remembers the Deacons for Defense, Charles and Roy Sims, Sam Barnes, and his grandfather Bill Crawford, sleeping armed atop the houses and ringing the women in the middle of marches, a night car chase when his father shot back through the mirror, and how fear of the Deacons stopped white vendors, one watermelon seller having his load burst melon by melon.

chapters.title: You're Going to Meet Your Mammy Today
chapters.summary: Chuck notes Monroe bought the store partly to move out a white neighbor who fancied Toni, and Toni describes her mother as secretary-treasurer of the Voters League quietly directing presidents A.Z. Young and Bob Hicks and serving twenty-seven years on the school board, where she once met a Genco family confrontation by pulling a .38 from her purse and laying it on the table, a measure of the blunt, profane fearlessness she brought to board meetings.

chapters.title: The Reason I Don't Tell You Is I Show You
chapters.summary: Toni and Geraldine describe Gayle's kindness toward poor people of any race, taking in her stepchildren, Chuck's friend, and Rickey Hill's sister Gilda, and the long ache that her mother never said she loved her until, answering Toni's letter, she explained that she showed it every day, a love Geraldine likened to fighting a bear for her children.

chapters.title: You Develop a Tunnel Vision
chapters.summary: Chuck recalls a march down Columbia Road when a white man named Applewhite swung a crowbar and the police ran him down, the crowd briefly fleeing back to Freedom Hall before marching out again through a thousand whites, Hattie Mae Hill's dress sprayed up by a hosepipe, and the tunnel vision of a thirteen-year-old who feared his mother more than the street.

chapters.title: Have You Ever Heard That Klansman
chapters.summary: Geraldine and the family recount the march toward Baton Rouge that left Bogalusa with twenty-five or thirty people, harassed every step while John Doar was sent down, and swelled to five thousand by the city, and the day outside Satsuma when troopers could not protect a march of about eighty-six from thousands of whites; the night before, Don Duane Exposé and others had taken razors from barbershops, and as whites jumped the cars the marchers slashed them, astonishing the Klan who had thought them unarmed.

chapters.title: The Bigger You Are, the Harder You Fall
chapters.summary: Toni and Chuck describe Don as humbuggish and a lover of trouble who led the first school walkout, recalling Gayle taking his gun from a confrontation, his refusal to let anyone speak ill of his mother and sister, ordering Gayle out of his house, and the time he shot the antenna off a car of whites chasing him on Columbia Street and told his mother he had only fired in the air.

chapters.title: Niggers Invading the Schools
chapters.summary: Chuck recounts integrating the school after Principal Flemings called it an invasion on the radio, being struck with a crutch in the hallway, returning with a borrowed pocketknife to beat the boy who hit him, and the principal warning him only to keep quiet about the knife, on the day the Klansmen surrounded the school and pulled a gun on A.Z. Young.

chapters.title: Figuring Out How to Survive
chapters.summary: Chuck describes the constant racial taunting from coaches and classmates, Coach Volk proposing they let the boys fight it out, beating Bob Thomas so badly the offer was withdrawn, and a high school spent in survival mode, never studying, only working out how to make it through the next day after a real education in the all-black grade school.

chapters.title: Why Is Your Name Exposé
chapters.topic: Barred From a Job at Home
chapters.summary: Toni explains the cost of loving Bogalusa yet being kept from working there, teaching in New Orleans and Picayune, Superintendent M.J. Israel demanding to know why her name was Exposé and her mother's Jenkins, and her years of denied principalships while less-qualified people received emergency certifications to keep the jobs from her.

chapters.title: Don't You Ever Tell Me to Sit Down
chapters.summary: Toni recounts Applewhite, from a Klan family, vowing she would have nothing, her defense of the first black superintendent he degraded, her demand that he never again tell her to sit down at a board meeting, the blackballing that followed, and how her mother's voting influence finally won her a behavior-discipline job as Applewhite lost his last race.

chapters.title: We Can Go to Any Restaurant Now
chapters.summary: Toni and Geraldine weigh what changed, integrated communities like Founders Drive, open restaurants, friendships across race, against Geraldine's own story of working twenty years for twenty dollars a week, raising six children, earning her RN, being barred from every Bogalusa hospital, and commuting twenty-eight miles to Covington for her whole career.

chapters.title: A Common Goal and a Common Enemy
chapters.summary: The family remembers being raised as one household with Aunt Jack, whose movement-bought Cadillac and house in the white community marked real change, the State and Ritz theaters that seated blacks upstairs, and Chuck's reflection that a common enemy once kept black neighbors from killing one another, though the churches stayed segregated then as now.

chapters.title: He Was at the House All the Time
chapters.summary: Asked about the police force, still brutal after a federal judge enjoined it in Hicks v. Knight, Toni recalls attorney Dick Sobol counseling the leaders at the house, the book Crossing Border Street about Gayle and Bob Hicks, and the young black New Orleans law firm of Robert Collins, Nils Douglas, and Lolis Elie tied to CORE, one of whom offered Chuck a place to stay and a Tulane scholarship he turned down.

chapters.title: Taken Out in a Casket
chapters.summary: The family recalls national figures passing through Bogalusa, and the night James Farmer, threatened with death, was smuggled out of town from Plaquemine in a casket through Richmond's funeral home, and how bringing celebrities drew the Klan from Poplarville and Franklinton, making the marches worse, while Gayle warned City Hall they would march permit or no permit.

chapters.title: Worry About Your Own Lights
chapters.summary: The family describes lining up to be loaded onto the paddy wagons and buses that hauled marchers to jail, and Dick Gregory speaking at the park by Sweetville Baptist Church when the lights went out, telling the crowd to worry about their own lights and telling the surrounding police they were as oppressed as the marchers and needed freedom too.

chapters.title: My Mama Told Me Don't Play With No Nigger Doll
chapters.summary: Toni shares her hope that people will look past skin color, the kindergarten white girl who refused a black doll and insisted Toni was white like her, and the story of Chuck's son Cal marrying a white girl over Gayle's furious objection and Corvette bribe, until a cousin reminded Gayle she had marched for equal rights, drawing the retort that she had not marched for that.

chapters.title: Opportunity Was the Name of the Game
chapters.summary: Toni frames education and opportunity as her mother's true goal, contrasts her own rich all-black high school years as school queen with Chuck's lonely integrated experience, and notes that whites now bus their children to Franklinton or private schools while the once all-white band and Paper Dolls dance team have become almost entirely black.

chapters.title: I Love You Because You're a Racist
chapters.summary: As the interview winds down the family disagrees over a street to be named for Gayle, Chuck preferring roses given while one lives, and Toni tells of Senator Ben Nevers, a racist board colleague Gayle walked the streets to defeat who returned on his knees to win her support then demanded a federal investigation of Chuck, of candidates coming through her chair, and of Gayle telling the openly racist principal Mr. Creel she loved him for letting her know where he stood, insisting that without the backbone, integration would never have come to Bogalusa.

chapters.title: What About My Sisters
chapters.summary: The family tells how Governor John McKeithen offered Gayle a fifty-thousand-dollar job to leave the movement, and how she turned it into a demand that every black woman in Bogalusa be given work, refusing the money, and Toni gives thanks for the chance to reflect on a mother she had feared was forgotten.

chapters.title: It Was the Last Thing in Her Will
chapters.summary: Answering Elaine Nichols, the family says Gayle was proudest of her children, who learned of her love only from her will, and Chuck recalls her freeing him from the racist deputy Vertrees Adams over a motorcycle, while Toni recounts her mother loving her people and wanting everyone equal, still political as she retired from the school board before dying that December.

chapters.title: She Burned It All in the Backyard
chapters.summary: The family closes with Gayle's indifference to appearance, the day her wig sat backwards at the board office, her wish only to help people live in harmony with education first, her pride in Toni's daughter as the family's first college graduate, and the day near the end when she burned all her movement papers, court orders, and photographs in the backyard while Richard Sobol was recording a book on her life.

loc_video.caption: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Bogalusa, Louisiana, 2011 May 28
